name: Vortex Energy
description: Vortex energy, in the context of alternative science, refers to the belief that certain locations on Earth are sites of concentrated energy flow, often described as spiraling or vortex-like. Proponents suggest these vortexes can have measurable effects on human health, consciousness, and even physical phenomena. The theory draws inspiration from various sources, including ancient geomancy, ley lines, and New Age spiritualism. Claims often involve enhanced spiritual experiences, healing properties, and unusual electromagnetic readings at these locations. Popular examples include Sedona, Arizona; Stonehenge, England; and Machu Picchu, Peru. Evidence cited by proponents includes anecdotal accounts of heightened awareness, personal healing experiences, and alleged anomalous readings from instruments like compasses and dowsing rods. Some proponents link vortex energy to free energy devices and suppressed technologies. Mainstream scientific perspective dismisses vortex energy as pseudoscience. Controlled experiments have failed to consistently demonstrate any measurable energy anomalies correlated with purported vortex locations. The anecdotal evidence is attributed to placebo effects, psychological suggestibility, and confirmation bias. Electromagnetic anomalies can be explained by geological features and human activity, not mysterious energy flows. The concept lacks a coherent theoretical framework supported by established physics and relies heavily on selective interpretation of data and unsubstantiated claims. The association with suppressed technologies further pushes it into conspiratorial territory.
subtitle: Energy hot spots or pseudoscience?

claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts of heightened awareness and spiritual insights at vortex sites.
claims.criticism: Experiences are subjective and attributable to psychological suggestibility, expectation bias, and the environment's ambiance.

claims.evidence: Personal testimonials of physical and emotional healing experienced at vortex locations.
claims.criticism: Healing claims are anecdotal and lack scientific validation; placebo effect and natural healing processes are more likely explanations.

claims.evidence: Anomalous compass readings, dowsing rod responses, and alleged electromagnetic field variations.
claims.criticism: Electromagnetic anomalies can be explained by geological formations, underground water sources, or human-made interference; dowsing has no scientific basis.

keyFigures.name: Richard Hoagland
keyFigures.role: Promoter of alternative theories, including connections to vortex energy and ancient civilizations.

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic who critiques pseudoscientific claims, including those related to vortex energy.

timeline.event: Geomancy practices and sacred site selection based on perceived Earth energies.
Alfred Watkins popularizes the concept of ley lines in England.
New Age movement adopts and expands the concept of vortex energy, associating it with spiritual and healing properties.

sources.author: David Hatcher Childress
sources.work: Lost Cities of North & Central America
sources.title: David Hatcher Childress
